name: pywin32 (Windows API Bindings for Python)

auth.notes: Uses Windows credentials implicitly; no explicit auth

performance.notes: Direct Win32 API calls; near-native performance

agent_readiness.notes: Windows API, COM, and WMI access from Python. import win32api; import win32con; import win32gui; import win32com.client; import winerror. win32api.GetComputerName(). win32api.RegOpenKey(). win32gui.FindWindow(None, 'Window Title'). win32gui.SendMessage(hwnd, WM_CLOSE, 0, 0). COM: shell = win32com.client.Dispatch('WScript.Shell'). shell.SendKeys('%{F4}'). Services: import win32service; sc = win32service.OpenSCManager(None, None, win32con.SC_MANAGER_ALL_ACCESS). WMI via wmi module (wraps pywin32). win32process for process management. win32security for ACLs. win32net for network operations. Used by many Windows automation tools. Windows only.

gotchas: Documentation is thin; often requires referencing Win32 MSDN docs
No type annotations — IDE support is weak
COM automation is powerful but complex and verbose
32-bit vs 64-bit Python matters for some COM components
best_when: Windows-specific automation: registry, services, processes, GUI
COM automation (Office, Internet Explorer, custom COM servers)
When ctypes Win32 would be more verbose
avoid_when: Simple Windows tasks where subprocess + PowerShell is sufficient
When winreg (stdlib) covers the registry needs
summary: pywin32 provides Windows API, COM, and WMI access from Python. import win32api; import win32gui; import win32com.client. win32gui.FindWindow(None, 'Title'); win32api.GetComputerName(); COM: win32com.client.Dispatch('WScript.Shell'). Windows only. PSF-2 by Mark Hammond. No type annotations — docs thin. Best for Windows automation, COM, services, registry. Use subprocess+PowerShell for simple tasks.
